title: Polio case and death rates in the United States
subtitle: The reported rates are per 100,000 US population and include both [wild-](#dod:wild-poliovirus) and [vaccine-derived poliovirus](#dod:vaccine-derived-poliovirus) infections that occurred indigenously and as imported cases.
caption:
Polio case and death rates in the United States

The reported rates are per 100,000 US population and include both wild- and vaccine-derived poliovirus infections that occurred indigenously and as imported cases.

The chart displays two line series tracking polio case and death rates in the United States from 1910 to 2023. The orange line represents the polio case rate, while the blue line indicates the polio death rate. Between 1910 and 1950, the case rate fluctuates significantly, with notable peaks around 1916, 1935, and 1945, reaching values between 5 and 15 per 100,000 population. A sharp increase occurs in the early 1950s, with the case rate peaking above 35 per 100,000 around 1952. Following this peak, the case rate declines rapidly, dropping below 5 per 100,000 by 1958 and approaching zero by 1960. The death rate remains consistently lower than the case rate throughout the period, with its highest point around 1916 at approximately 7 per 100,000. After 1960, both rates remain near zero through 2023. The data includes both wild- and vaccine-derived poliovirus infections that occurred indigenously and as imported cases, measured per 100,000 US population.
